paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: The deeper you dig into Costco, the more good things you find. This company seems simple on the face of it—sell in bulk and offer great deals. But what really makes it work are 50 clever innovations that they've refined over the years that all work together like an orchestra that's been rehearsing for decades. Nothing about Costco is an accident, from the extra wide parking spaces to the whole rotisserie chickens.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: There's a Warren Buffett joke about Costco: Warren and Charlie are flying on a plane that gets hijacked. The hijackers grant each one last wish. Charlie says, I would like to give my speech on the virtues of Costco one more time before I die. Then the hijackers turn to Warren and he says, shoot me first. This actually happened at a Berkshire annual meeting. It's on YouTube.

paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: Here are some insane stats. Costco has grown revenue right about 10 percent for over 30 years in a row. Their revenue per square foot of their warehouses belongs more in a conversation with Tiffany than Walmart. Their store brand, Kirkland Signature, does more revenue alone than all of Nike. It's 52 billion a year, which inches by Nike by just about a billion dollars.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Costco was founded in Seattle in 1983 by retail veterans Jim Sinegal and Jeffrey Brotman. But the true history dates way further back to Sol Price and his two companies, FedMart and Price Club. The organization that we know and love today is actually the result of a merger between Costco and Price Club. And Price Club is really the result of Sol's previous company, FedMart.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Sol Price was born in January 1916 in New York City in the Bronx. His parents were Jewish immigrants from Belarus who arrived at Ellis Island as teenagers with absolutely nothing. They spoke no English, they had no money. Sol's parents ended up getting jobs in the garment factories in the Lower East Side. The conditions were terrible. Sol says, in the New York Jewish community at the time, there was no such thing as Republicans. The socialists were the conservatives and the communists were the radicals.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: In the middle of his high school years, Sol's parents moved the family from New York City to San Diego, California, a town of like one hundred and fifty thousand people. Sol meets his future wife Helen while he's in high school. Helen comes from one of the wealthiest families in San Diego. Her family owns and operates a scrap metal business, which was perfectly positioned because San Diego was about to boom during World War Two as the principal port of the U.S. Navy's Pacific Fleet.

paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: One of Sol's clients as a lawyer is called the Seven Seas Locker Club, which ostensibly was lockers where Navy sailors could store their uniforms when they're on leave. But actually, that's just a Trojan horse to get consumers into the door. They offer all kinds of goods and services: laundry, dry cleaning, clothing, jewelry, food, haircuts. Another client is Four Star Jewelers, who sell jewelry wholesale to a retail concept in Los Angeles called Fedco.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Fedco was a nonprofit membership club, a customer collective. It was called Fedco because it was only open to federal employees, primarily postal workers. About 800 postal workers in the Los Angeles area decided to pool their buying power together. They charged a membership fee of five dollars one time for a lifetime Fedco membership. Fedco becomes quite popular. People start driving from all over Southern California, sometimes up to hundreds of miles round trip to do the majority of their shopping at Fedco.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Sol calls up the Fedco board of directors and says, we want to partner with you guys. We think that a Fedco would do great in San Diego. Can we create a joint venture together? Fedco turns him down. Sol calls them back and says, you can own the whole business. We'll just be a franchise. You get all the upside. We don't care. They say no again because it's a nonprofit board of directors. After this second rejection, Sol and the guys are like, I guess we now can go do it ourselves.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: In November 1954, they open the store and call it FedMart. Why do you think Walmart is called Walmart? Why do you think Kmart was called Kmart? It's because of this. By the time Walmart was starting, people knew what FedMarts were. Sam Walton talks about this in Made in America. He was like, oh great, we're going to draft off the FedMart brand. FedMart becomes the first scaled quasi-national discounter.

paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: The reason they did this and
